text: The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon tracing the leads of a gastric neurostimulator as they enter the greater curvature of the stomach.

The surgeon identifies the permanent suture securing the leads to the greater curvature of the stomach and proceeds to take down adhesions to restore normal anatomical configuration. The site of a previously removed jejunostomy tube is visible thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz.

The surgeon uses a stapler to detach the small bowel from the abdominal wall, ensuring the lumen remains wide. An open incision is made over the jejunostomy tube site to remove the remnant staple line and small intestine. The biliopancreatic limb is visible, with the jejunostomy tube site located approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon identifies a small hiatal hernia during the gastric bypass procedure, which was previously noted on preoperative endoscopy, and prepares to repair it.
The surgeon creates a second gastrotomy for the placement of the anvil, revealing a moderate amount of gastric fluid within the stomach, likely due to the patient's gastroparesis.

The surgeon uses a finger dissector to create a tunnel for the stapler amidst dense adhesions posterior to the stomach. The gastric stimulator leads are visible and could interfere with the gastrojejunostomy anastomosis. However, the Roux limb is successfully positioned medially to the leads for the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative endoscopy to evaluate the gastric pouch and conduct a leak test. The omentum is mobilized and placed underneath the gastric stimulator leads to prevent them from resting on top of the Roux limb. The final view shows the gastric stimulator and the Roux limb in their respective positions.
